speaker: interviewer
text: '95, interview with Sol Urbach, tape number five. You were talking about the night of May 7, but before we get to that is there anything else about your life in Brunnlitz that you might want to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- in fact, I do. One of the memorable things that happened during that six-month stay in Brunnlitz is that at one point in time-- and I, again, cannot place it exactly in the timing-- but it was sometime in 1945-- that into that small camp of Brunnlitz, Amon Goeth arrived, the same killer that we left over in Krakow-Plaszow. And we thought that he disappeared and is gone from our life. Lo and behold, he appeared there. And I saw him walking through the factory building, but it was not the same Amon Goeth. He must have been-- after he lost a great amount of weight, the uniform hung on him. It did not fit him anymore. He was stripped of his ranks. There was no longer any spic and span in him. And as we know now, there is a good chance that Amon Goeth came out of jail, arriving to Schindler if Schindler could help him out for the rest of the war. But I remember him a totally different man-- thin, losing a lot of weight, uniform hanging on him, and no decorations anymore than what-- what I remembered seeing him in Krakow-Plaszow. And in Krakow-Plaszow, I did have occasions to see him close up as he walked by on the street. On occasion, I saw him walking with Mietek Pemper discussing-- Mietek Pemper was a sort of a Jewish secretary to Amon Goeth. He was an intellectual and educated person, evidently. And Amon Goeth took a liking to him, and he had him be his secretary. I saw him also close up on occasions when lynchings were going on all around the appellplatz. So I knew Amon Goeth by sight at that time. So when he arrived in Brunnlitz, it was a total shock. But thank god he was not the same commander, and he had no influence on our lives anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It does set you back. It-- you-- you swallow and-- and wonder, I mean, are we going backwards, or is the clock still moving into the right direction? There is no question, but it sort of makes you wonder.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at Brunnlitz, you were already a young man. Did you have friends of the same age? Did you meet any girls in the camp, or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Brunnlitz itself, yes, we had the luxury of talking to one another. The great fear of the concentration camp was dissipating slowly. It was not a total free-for-all. I couldn't go around the whole camp and shake hands with all the inmates in the camp. I couldn't do that. As a matter of fact, if I could've, I would've found out if there was another Urbach by the name of David, first name. But I didn't. But on the other hand, I developed [? many other ?] friendships with people my age. I became very close friends with a-- with people that worked together with me in the workshop. And some of the entries that were made in my memoir when the-- when the war ended tell the story of how we felt towards-- how these people felt towards me. And the particular friendship that I developed was with a girl by the name of Anita Lampel. You could not call it a girlfriend-boyfriend, but we were certainly on a talking-- in a talking relationship, friendly with one another. Olek Allerhand, who was in Israel; Anita Lampel, who was-- later on remained in Poland, educated herself, and then eventually migrated to Israel, lived in Israel, now deceased; Adel Mandel he was to-- who was described by me before, but did not survive in Brunnlitz, also deceased. He fought in six wars in Israel, and he is now deceased too; Olek Allerhand, who did survive with me, is in Israel now. And there were a bunch of others. There was a fellow by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. We were all young. Bejski-- this is the brother of Justice Bejski of Israel-- was a close friend of mine. And he was the-- this friend of mine, by the name of Bejski-- and I don't remember his first name at the moment-- but-- but he was the valet for Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a close circle of people that we kept in touch with. And we-- in fact, after Schindler declared that we are free people, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people began to think on what we do next. I walked around with this memoir, getting people to sign their names so I would remember them a bit longer.

speaker: interviewee
text: We began to scheme on what we do from now on. And a group of people-- one of them, the leader was one of the people that worked with me in the woodworking shop-- began to put together a group of people that would take off after a few days and go towards Krakow, Poland. And the intention of that group was to do it quickly opposed to some people who remained in the camp. Number one, some of them were not strong enough. Number two, some of them hesitated to go back to Poland. This group was planning to go to Poland. In retrospect, looking at it and thinking about this plan, it was a poorly hatched plan, again, because we had no idea where we were. And if I look at the route that this group took from Brunnlitz to go to Krakow, we went north instead of heading east all the time. So we really did not know. We were not prepared intellectually, mentally to do it. But nevertheless, a group of 17 people-- 4 ladies and 13 men-- banded together after a few days after the end of the war. We banded together with the expressed purpose of reaching Krakow, Poland to see whether any of our relatives remained alive. This was our-- We did not know much about our will to stay in Poland. We had an idea that we don't, but nevertheless, we felt that this was our obligation is to get back to Krakow, Poland and begin to see if any of the relatives remained alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the 17 of us took risks, because we are now supposed to be going in Czechoslovakia heading eastward. The Russian army is marching westward. So they are greeting us. As they are going one way, we are going the other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way-- the only way we can make it to get to Krakow was on-- on foot and rounding up some horses. This is the end of the war, and there were some stray horses roaming the fields. So we were able to round up some of those horses that were limping a little bit or were not in the best of shape. But nevertheless, they would be good enough for us. And we would hitch them up to a wagon. And now we had those bundles that Oskar Schindler left for us in the warehouses. And we-- 17 of us would load up those bundles. Some of us that were weaker would be on the wagon. Some of us would walk. And we walked from-- from Brunnlitz towards Krakow. And we wound up in places that we should have never been. They were just totally off the map for where we were going. And after numerous days, maybe as many as seven days, we arrived in the city of Wroclaw. The 17 of us, in relatively good health by now-- the way we fed ourselves is by stopping off at farmers' and begging for food and, if necessary, stealing food. The only danger we faced was from the Russians. The Russians had absolutely no heart for the people that were wanderers. And so we were in danger with them. Well, I also have to back off now to-- to get back to the-- when Schindler left the camp and left us alone. Is that-- as Schindler predicted and told us on May 8, 1945, a single Russian soldier marched into our camp. And he was walking by a horse. That horse, at that time, when I first saw him, appeared like a little donkey. And the Russian soldier appeared like a 20-foot tall man. This was all-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just the moment created such a mirage that I saw things that never happened. When Schindler declared that the war is ended, the-- the cries and laughs and the push of the inmates toward Schindler to kiss his feet, to kiss his hands, to hug him, to squeeze him, were-- resulted in a totally indescribable situation. I mean, it was-- it was like something that you would-- you'll never see again in your life, what happened then, the reaction of people that we are finally free people. But Schindler left us, and as I started telling, we left on that trip. And after seven days of walking on foot, riding with horses, feeding ourselves by stealing and begging, we arrived in Wroclaw, Poland, where the city of Wroclaw was totally destroyed by the bombings. And when we arrived there, we-- in fact, in that city we were able to sell the two horses that never belong to us anyway. But we were able to sell them to have some pennies to get on a train from there on to go to Krakow. And from Wroclaw to Krakow, we went by train to when we arrived in Krakow a few days later. And it must have still been June-- early June when I arrived in Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And upon arrival in Krakow, this was probably one of my saddest days, because here I am in the city of Krakow, no place to go. I have absolutely no knowledge of how you go about finding anybody, any family. I would have to go back to-- to my home and see if anybody arrived there. But immediately-- right there and then-- thinking about it from Czechoslovakia, this was all possible. You go and look. But when I arrived in the city, I all of the sudden found myself alone, and more so because in that group of 17 people, mostly everybody found a place to go to. I actually wound up on the street of Krakow with almost no place to go, until one of the people that was with me together on the transport offers that I better go with her until I find something. So when I got to the city was a-- city of Krakow was an extremely sad moment. I almost felt like this was all in vain because it doesn't look like I'll be finding anybody. Of course, after a number of days, housing was assigned to us in a short while. Polish government assigned to us a former student building for housing of those people that were arriving. And I lived in the student building in Krakow. We did not know what we will do with our lives from here on, but, again, the situation developed that the Poles were drafting people into the army. And I realized that being the age of 20 at that time-- close to 20. I was 19-- that I was subject to draft. Now, I did a little quick thinking and realized that if I made myself one year younger, since I had no documents on me-- I have absolutely no documents. Nobody knows where I was born-- if I made myself one year younger and established this in some kind of a document, I might escape the army temporarily until I leave the country. And sure enough, that worked. I was never drafted into the army. And-- and I began living in Krakow for a short while. I developed a skill of being able to cross the borders illegally. And I went to Czechoslovakia with the attempt to bring some goods from Czechoslovakia to Poland, so I would sell this and make some money, so I would be able to support myself, because immediately after the war, there was no organization functioning that would help you, dish out even food. There was no Polish agency. There was no UNRRA. There was no-- there were no Jewish organizations functioning that quickly after the war to really guide us, help us, or anything. After a number of days, there were Jewish organizations that began to function. There were some attempts made to-- to-- permit us to submit names of people to the gathering places, so everybody would know if some relatives would arrive. But over the next few weeks while I was in-- in Krakow, Poland, I did not find anyone. I went back to my home where we lived before the war. I did not find anyone. And after only a number of weeks after that, I was together with this girl, Anita Lampel, who was my close friend at that time. And we discovered that a transport is arriving from Mauthausen, and we had an idea that one of our buddies, Adam Mandel, whom I described before, was arriving in this-- possibly with the transport from Mauthausen. And so we went to that school. Both Anita and myself went to the school to search for this Adam Mandel when he arrives on this transport. And these people that arrived from Mauthausen were in such bad shape physically, almost emaciated people, nothing but skin and bones, that there was no way of recognizing. If I was looking for this Adam Mandel, I would have not recognized him. So I devised quickly a way that as we go through that room, we keep calling out, Adam, Adam, Adam. And we walked through these bodies of people who were sitting on the floor, not able really to stand up, until one figure began to unwind from the floor. And he was a tall man, but because he was so thin-- nothing but skin on him, and bones-- at that time, he looked to me like a tremendously tall man by the time he unwound. And this was the fellow, Adam Mandel that arrived from the transport of-- so this was the closest person that I found that I could somebody I know close, outside of my family, because no one in my family was left alive. From the liquidation on, of the ghetto, I never found anyone alive. And later on, we developed a-- a sort of a friendship with Adam, where we continued together. We-- shortly thereafter, we left from Poland and went to Czechoslovakia, thinking that in Czechoslovakia we will escape communism, because we didn't really know the political situation that well. We were youngsters. But we discovered quickly that-- and we did like the Czechoslovak people. The Czechoslovak people are extremely friendly people. And we thought Czechoslovakia was the place to settle. And we did settle for awhile, until maybe the end of summer of 1945. At the end of summer, we recognized that the-- that the Czechoslovakia is equally occupied by the Russians, and this is also not a safe place to-- to stay. And we-- again, there was no-- no functioning organizations to take you any place yet. This was too early, a little too early. And so we had to be geniuses, just like Schindler had to be a genius, to cross the borders. Now, all of the sudden I found myself in that same situation, that I had to be a genius to cross illegally borders. I had to cross from Poland into Czechoslovakia illegally. In fact, I went with a Russian soldier. I discovered that there is some place a Russian soldier that, for a bottle of vodka, he'll take you on a-- on a big truck that he holds potatoes on. He's got the potatoes covered with straw. You go under that straw, and he takes you right through the Polish border. And the Polish guards will not dare asking a Russian whether he has any passengers or not. And that's how I cross the border from Poland into Czechoslovakia. Then from Czechoslovakia, I had to continue. I had to cross all of Czechoslovakia-- the full length, and get to the part of West Germany. And at that time I also crossed the border. By simply-- I mean, fear was just not in me anymore. I felt that maybe the world owes me something at that time. And I actually got on a mountain, where I climbed the mountain, and over the mountain, I came into West Germany, where I settled in Bamberg. And from then on, together with Adam Mandel and then some other friends by the name of Szymek Szajn and-- and Karl Diamond, we formed a kind of a family unit, where we lived in Bamberg, Germany. In Bamberg, we arrived still in October of 1945. And when we arrived there, the Germans were not hostile at all towards us. We wanted to be there because the Americans were there, and we knew that they would provide us with a kind of a security blanket. But the Germans were extremely accommodating at that time. And they-- and most of them did not know anything about what their compatriots or what their family members did during the war-- that in fact, many people were killed. They were extremely anxious to-- to do anything they can for us. A German landlady gave up her two rooms for us to settle in her house. And we were the guests of the German landlady in Bamberg. Not the-- she did not receive us with open arms, but nevertheless, she did permit us into the house, and that's where we lived for the duration of the next four years. In the four years, I begin to think that, in fact, I have an uncle and aunt that might be in the United States. As a confused youngster, at 13 when the war broke out, normal thinking was not part of my life anymore from the age of 13 on to the age of 18. So I didn't know much about relatives, where they all are, what their names are, what their birthdays are. I just had no knowledge. This was just too long of a span of a young man-- in a young man's time to know any of that. But I did recall that my mother had a brother in the United States and therefore his name would have been Baldinger. And my mother's name is Baldinger, B-A-L-D-I-N-G-E-R. And so I began to look for this Baldinger. And sometime in 1947, my uncle from the United States began to search equally records on whether any of the Baldingers were alive. And we made contact. I made contact with him. And we began to correspond. And he secured an affidavit in the United States, where he found-- he was not a man of great means. But he did find an employer who was willing to sign an affidavit that I would be permitted to come to this country, and I would not be a burden on the country if he brought me over. He would-- this employer would give me work and all that, and even that didn't help. You think-- you wonder today how people can come into this country so easy, and in those days, we could not come into this country until 1949. The four-- the four years in Germany I utilized to continue my education. A German professor was willing to sit down with me and bring me up through high school education. I found a man that was-- I started working for as a radio mechanic. But until 1949, it was all a dream that one day we will be able to leave Germany. We certainly did not-- the last country in the world we wanted to stay in was Germany. And it was not until 1949, and the affidavit that my uncle has prepared had no impact on it, it was Truman who permitted 200,000 displaced persons to come into the United States. And I came under that quota to the United States in 1949. In March of 1949, the ship that brought us here, the boat that brought us here, the name was USS Jumper. And in March-- March 13, I arrived in this country. As you remember March 13, three years ago-- not three years ago, six years ago, was the liquidation of the ghetto. So March 13 was once again a very important date in my life when I arrived in this country. I arrived in this country, and my uncle and aunt that lived at that time Passaic, New Jersey, was willing to have me in their home. And I began searching for work. Again, I began searching for work at that time as a radio mechanic, but it was not easy to get jobs. At that time, the GIs were returning from war. Jobs were hard to come by. The survivors of concentration camps did not rate any better or different from anybody else. And we had to compete for jobs. In fact, i couldn't find a job, because to work in a factory that was making radios, you had to be a citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't find a job. Eventually I found a job as, of all the things in the world, in a place that was doing enamel work. And lo and behold, in the United States, in Paterson, New Jersey, I was back in front of an oven, generating 1,000 degrees of heat when the door was opened. So I realized that this is the worse of the two evils, and maybe I should go back and look if I can't go to the trade of carpentry, which I had some exposure to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some carpentry and cabinet making experience. So I gravitated into that, and I began to work in a furniture shop in Paterson, New Jersey. I got full employment at that time, and received, in 1949-- when I began working in Paterson New Jersey, I worked for an outfit by the name of-- well, Gordon Brothers was the second employer. There was another one whose name I don't remember. But it was the furniture making business, and my wages were $0.75 an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember my cousin with whom I lived at the time asking me, well, are you now a happy man earning $0.75 an hour? And I said, yes, I'm extremely happy. I'm doing extremely well. He says, well, how much money would you need to make to be really happy-like? I said, if I ever made $50 a week, I would be the happiest man alive. And his answer was, you will probably make $50 and a lot more. And I don't know whether you'll be the happiest man.
